This one's in from Holly Tar. I do all phone sales, so my leads come in from an ad, and then I need to get them on the phone. What is the best way to build certainty through a voicemail? How can I get somebody to respond to my voicemail or first email to send an appointment to speak with them? So voicemails, so I've gone back and forth this and I've tested out all different types of language patterns for voicemails, right? There's two routes to this. The first route is to be evasive and pretend you're a customer. So I never want you to lie, but go up to the point, so leave a message that would almost make them think that they're calling back a customer, then you'll get a lot of callbacks, right? Like for instance, in a logistics field, if you leave a message, I'm calling you about a law, about a package that you're, a package that I know, I'm calling about a package delivery. Like they think that you wanna get a package delivered when actually, so you're calling the head of transmission, yeah, I'm calling about a package delivery, calling back at XYZ, you know, 818, you get it? Well, you get a lot of callbacks there, but they might be a little pissed when they call you back because you position it like it's a call. So you test that out versus where you say, hey, it's a Jordan calling from Mike, give me a callback and just say the number, but make it quick, short, and play the numbers game. Don't leave long-winded messages. With the last thing you wanna do, do not try to call someone on a voicemail, the more you say the worse it gets. Just quick, get them to call you back and then test that out versus not leaving messages on voicemails. And this again is how many, do you have a steady flow of leads coming in? If you're short of leads, you'll tend to always wanna call and leave messages, but if you have enough leads, you're not gonna wanna do it because what happens is the time you spend leaving a voicemail, writing it down, keeping track of it, you're gonna just make more calls.
